request.question: Who was the singer of She's Not Just a Pretty Face and Forever and for Always?

title: Impatient (Anna Abreu song)
Dolly Sinatra
She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Just a Pretty Face?
Forever and for Always
Face Facts
On to Victory (album)
Never Trust a Pretty Face
The Pretty Things Are Going to Hell
Pretty Face
sentences: "Impatient" is a song by Finnish singer Anna Abreu from her third studio album, "Just a Pretty Face?
Abreu co-wrote the song with longtime collaborator Rauli Eskolin (known professionally as Rake), and Patric Sarin.
Eskolin also produced the song, while Sarin provided additional backing vocals.
"Impatient" is a Pop ballad.
The song was released on 9 November 2009 in Finland, as the album's second single.
Dolly Sinatra (born Natalina Maria Vittoria Garaventa; December 26, 1896 – January 6, 1977) was the mother of American singer Frank Sinatra.
She was born in Lumarzo (Province of Genoa), in northern Italy; she immigrated to the United States when she was two months old.
When she was a child, her pretty face earned her the nickname "Dolly".
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.
It was the fourth country single from her album "Up!
The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.
Twain had announced that "Nah!"
would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.
It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.
The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.
It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.
Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.
The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.
The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.
Just a Pretty Face?
is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.
The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
"Forever and for Always" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain and it was the third country single from her fourth studio album "Up!
The song was written by Mutt Lange and Twain.
The single was released in the United States radio on April 7, 2003.
The video debuted on Country Music Television on April 26, 2003.
The song was also used for Febreze: Scentstories promotion, in which Twain took part.
In 2006, "Forever and for Always" was certified gold for 500,000 digital downloads in the U.S. by the RIAA.
Face Facts is a 1980 album from glam rock band T. Roth and Another Pretty Face.
Bon Jovi drummer Tico Torres plays on the album.

On to Victory is the ninth studio album recorded by the English rock band Humble Pie and the first with a new lineup including vocalist and guitarist Steve Marriott, Drummer Jerry Shirley, vocalist and guitarist Bobby Tench from The Jeff Beck Group, and American bassist Anthony "Sooty" Jones.
They recorded "Fool for a Pretty Face", which Marriott had written earlier and the song proved good enough for them to secure a recording contract with Atco in 1980.
In UK their material was released by Jet Records, owned by the former Small Faces manager Don Arden.

The album peaked #60 on the "Billboard" 200 album chart "Fool for a Pretty Face" was released as a single and reached #58 on the US singles charts and a promotional tour followed as part of "The Rock'n Roll Marathon", supporting Ted Nugent and Aerosmith
Never Trust a Pretty Face is the third studio album by French singer Amanda Lear, released by the West German label Ariola Records in 1979.
The album included notable hit singles "The Sphinx" and "Fashion Pack (Studio 54)", and turned out a commercial and critical success.
"The Pretty Things Are Going to Hell" is a song written by David Bowie and Reeves Gabrels for the album "Hours" in 1999.
The first single release from the album in Australia and Japan, while the rest of the world got "Thursday's Child" as their first single.
The first appearance of the song was on the soundtrack of the film "Stigmata" in 1999.
It charted and peaked at No. 30 in Japan.
The song's title takes influence from the song "Your Pretty Face Is Going to Hell" by The Stooges from their album "Raw Power" produced by Bowie himself.
Pretty Face (プリティフェイス , Puriti Feisu ) is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Japanese author Yasuhiro Kano.

The story revolves around a high school boy who suffers an accident and having his face reconstructed in the form of his crush, attempts to impersonate a girl whom his crush mistakes for her twin sister.
A common theme in the series is the loving relationships the characters share for each other, whether they be between siblings, friends, or lovers as well as Karma.
It was first serialized in Jump Comic's "Weekly Shōnen Jump" by Shueisha between May 2002 and June 2003, finally coming to an end at chapter fifty-two.
For North America Viz Media acquired the rights to release an English translation of the series.

title: She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Forever and for Always

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Who was the singer of She's Not Just a Pretty Face and Forever and for Always?
hop1_docs: She's Not Just a Pretty Face | "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.  It was the fourth country single from her album "Up! ".  The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.  Twain had announced that "Nah!"  would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.  It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.  The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.  It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.  "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.  Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.  The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.  The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.

Just a Pretty Face? | Just a Pretty Face?  is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.  The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
Not Just a Pretty Face (Rowan Atkinson album) | Not Just a Pretty Face is a comedy album by British comedian Rowan Atkinson.

Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces | Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces is the seventh studio album by British jazz-rock band If, released in 1974.  The band was now back on Capitol Records for U.S. distribution.
Always is Forever Tour | Always is Forever Tour was a concerts tour by American singer Diana Ross in 1999.

Always & Forever (Randy Travis album) | Always & Forever is the second studio album by country music singer Randy Travis.  It was released on April 4, 1987 by Warner Bros.  Records.  Released from this album were the singles "Too Gone Too Long", "I Won't Need You Anymore (Always and Forever)", "Forever and Ever, Amen" and "I Told You So", all of which reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs charts.

Always and Forever (Kostas Martakis song) | "Always and Forever" is a song and CD single by Greek singer Kostas Martakis.  It was Martakis's entry in a race to be the Greek representative for the Eurovision Song Contest 2008.

response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Shania Twain; songs "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" and "Forever and for Always".

- "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" — "co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain." (passage [1])
- "'She's Not Just a Pretty Face' was the fourth country single from her album 'Up!'." (passage [1])
- "'She's Not Just a Pretty Face' debuted in North America on October 6, 2003." (passage [1])
- Passage states: "Twain had announced that 'Nah!' would be the next single after 'Forever and for Always'," (passage [1]) — this links "Forever and for Always" to Twain in the passage but does not explicitly state "'Forever and for Always' is a song recorded by Shania Twain" as a standalone declarative fact.
- Missing/ambiguous fact: explicit confirmation that "'Forever and for Always'" is recorded by Shania Twain (bridge entity: the song "Forever and for Always" or the album "Up!").

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. "Forever and for Always (song)" — lede/infobox and "Release" or "Credits" sections (to confirm artist and release details).
2. "Shania Twain" — lede/infobox and "Singles" or "Discography" sections (to confirm both songs are listed under her recordings).
3. "Up! (Shania Twain album)" — track listing and singles section (to confirm both songs appear on the album and single release order).
4. Search keywords if needed: "Forever and for Always Shania Twain song", "She's Not Just a Pretty Face Shania Twain" for alternate pages or single-release notes.
response.hop2_query: Forever and for Always Shania Twain recorded by
hop2_docs: Forever and for Always | "Forever and for Always" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain and it was the third country single from her fourth studio album "Up! " (2002).  The song was written by Mutt Lange and Twain.  The single was released in the United States radio on April 7, 2003.  The video debuted on Country Music Television on April 26, 2003.  The song was also used for Febreze: Scentstories promotion, in which Twain took part.  In 2006, "Forever and for Always" was certified gold for 500,000 digital downloads in the U.S. by the RIAA.

Shoes (Shania Twain song) | "Shoes" is a song performed by Canadian singer Shania Twain.  It was the first single released from the "Desperate Housewives soundtrack" in 2005.  The song was written by Twain and then-husband, Mutt Lange, and co-written by Tammy Hyler, Joie Scott and Kim Tribble.  It is the last studio recording issued by Twain prior to the dissolution of her marriage to Lange, and the last Twain recording to feature any involvement from Lange.  The song was originally written and recorded for use on the ABC television program "Desperate Housewives".

Shania Now Tour | The Shania Now Tour is planned to be the fourth concert tour by Canadian recording artist Shania Twain, in support of her fifth studio album "Now" (2017).  The tour is planned for 2018, with the goal of beginning in Tacoma on May 3, and concluding in London on October 2.  The tour will mark Twain's first since 2015's Rock This Country Tour, which, at the time, Twain announced would be her final run out on the road.

Shania Twain (album) | Shania Twain is the self-titled debut studio album by Shania Twain, released on April 20, 1993.  Although not a hit when originally released, Twain's subsequent successful albums gained her legions of fans, which generated more interest in "Shania Twain" causing it to be certified platinum in the US for shipments of one million copies.  None of the album's songs were included in Twain's "Greatest Hits" album, released in 2004.

She's Not Just a Pretty Face | "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.  It was the fourth country single from her album "Up! ".  The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.  Twain had announced that "Nah!"  would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.  It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.  The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.  It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.  "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.  Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.  The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.  The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.

Shania Twain videography | This is a list of Shania Twain music videos.  The list contains all thirty three music videos, with their respective directors, filming locations and release dates.  Also included is a list of alternate versions of each music video as Twain's music appeals to both country and pop markets.  All of the videos released from "Shania Twain", "The Woman in Me" and "Come on Over" are available on Twain's "Platinum Collection" DVD, with the exception of "When".  Videos from "Up!"  and "Greatest Hits" are available on select enhanced CD singles and a special DVD-Audio version of "Up!" .  Many of Twain's music videos have won various awards, see Shania Twain awards for more information.

Shania Twain Centre | The Shania Twain Centre was a city-owned tourist attraction that operated in Timmins, Ontario from 2001 to 2013.  It exhibited memorabilia, awards and other personal items loaned to the Centre by singer Shania Twain, who was raised in Timmins.
hop2_titles: She's Not Just a Pretty Face
response.summary_2: Both "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" and "Forever and for Always" were co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain. Shania Twain co-wrote both songs with Mutt Lange and released them as singles from her 2002 album Up!.
